From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

English Member of Parliament

| Sir Christopher Calthorpe | |
| --- | --- |
| Born | c. 1645 |
| Died | 7 February 1718 |
| Spouse | Dorothy Spring |
| Parent(s) | James Calthorpe, Catherine Lewkenor |

**Sir Christopher Calthorpe** [KB](/wiki/Order_of_the_Bath "Order of the Bath") (c. 1645 – 7 February 1718) was an [English](/wiki/England "England") [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament "Member of Parliament").[[1]](#cite_note-HOP-1)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christopher_Calthorpe&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Calthorpe was born into a rich [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") family which had held manorial property in [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia") since 1376 and had first represented the county in Parliament under [Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI_of_England "Henry VI of England"). His father was a [Parliamentary](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") sympathiser in the [Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") and had held local office under the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England"). His mother, Catherine, was the daughter of Sir Edward Lewkenor of Denham. Calthorpe was educated at [Christ's College, Cambridge](/wiki/Christ%27s_College,_Cambridge "Christ's College, Cambridge"), followed by [Middle Temple](/wiki/Middle_Temple "Middle Temple") in 1660. He was invested as a Knight of the [Order of the Bath](/wiki/Order_of_the_Bath "Order of the Bath") on 23 April 1661.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Political career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christopher_Calthorpe&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political career")]

Calthorpe joined the Norfolk [Tory](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)") political faction headed by [Robert Paston, 1st Earl of Yarmouth](/wiki/Robert_Paston,_1st_Earl_of_Yarmouth "Robert Paston, 1st Earl of Yarmouth"), which secured his election to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_England "House of Commons of England") in February 1679, representing [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Norfolk (UK Parliament constituency)"). However, the legitimacy of his election was challenged by his political rivals, and a by-election was held in May the same year.[[1]](#cite_note-HOP-1) It was won by the Whig candidate, [Sir John Hobart, 3rd Baronet](/wiki/Sir_John_Hobart,_3rd_Baronet "Sir John Hobart, 3rd Baronet"). In 1688, Calthorpe bluntly declared that he could not give his assent to the repeal of the [Test Act](/wiki/Test_Act "Test Act") and [Penal Laws](/wiki/English_Penal_Laws "English Penal Laws"), and he was removed from local office as a [Justice of the Peace](/wiki/Justice_of_the_Peace "Justice of the Peace"). A non-juror after the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution"), he was disarmed in September 1689 at the behest of his rival, Sir Henry Hobart, and in the following summer he was placed in confinement as a person ‘suspected to be dangerous to the peace of the kingdom’. Hobart again had him arrested after the [Jacobite assassination plot 1696](/wiki/Jacobite_assassination_plot_1696 "Jacobite assassination plot 1696"). He died in 1718.[[1]](#cite_note-HOP-1)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christopher_Calthorpe&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

He married Dorothy, the youngest daughter of [Sir William Spring, 1st Baronet](/wiki/Sir_William_Spring,_1st_Baronet "Sir William Spring, 1st Baronet"), in 1664, with whom he had five sons and nine daughters.[[1]](#cite_note-HOP-1)
